documentation.title: Introduction
documentation.description: Start with the basics of modern Angular.

children.title: What is Angular?
children.content: Angular is a web development framework for building modern apps. Every component must have: A TypeScript class with behaviors, an HTML template, and a CSS selector. You provide Angular-specific information via the @Component decorator. It provides a suite of tools for developers to build, test, and update their code with ease. Angular is built on TypeScript and provides high-quality libraries for routing, forms, client-server communication, and more. It emphasizes testability, maintainability, and strong typing through TypeScript. The Angular ecosystem includes a CLI, schematics, a robust router, and an extensive set of official libraries to accelerate development.

codeExamples.title: Hello World Component

documentation.title: Components
documentation.description: Building blocks of your Angular application interface.

children.title: Anatomy of a component
children.content: Components are the building blocks of Angular applications. They consist of a TypeScript class, an HTML template, and CSS styles. Every component defines a CSS selector that determines how it is used in HTML. Angular matches selectors statically at compile-time. The @Component decorator defines metadata such as the selector and template. Components encourage separation of concerns, enabling encapsulation of template, style, and behavior. Properly designed components are reusable, testable, and easy to reason about.

codeExamples.title: Basic Component
Profile Photo Component

children.title: Component selectors
children.content: Selectors determine how components are used in templates. Every component defines a CSS selector that determines how it is used in HTML. Angular matches selectors statically at compile-time. Angular supports type selectors, attribute selectors, and class selectors. Custom element names are recommended. Choosing the right selector improves clarity and avoids collisions with standard HTML or third-party libraries. Prefer element selectors with a consistent prefix for library components.

sections.title: When to use an attribute selector

codeExamples.title: Attribute Selector

children.title: Accepting data with input properties
children.content: Components specify data they accept by declaring inputs using the `input()` function. This returns an `InputSignal` which is read-only. Inputs allow data to flow from parent to child components. Modern Angular uses the input() function for signal-based inputs, which are read-only and observable. Inputs should be treated as immutable from the child's perspective; derive computed values instead of mutating inputs. Document required inputs and types clearly to aid consumers and avoid runtime surprises.

children.title: Custom events with outputs
children.content: Angular components define custom events by assigning a property to the `output()` function. You can emit events by calling the `.emit()` method. Outputs allow child components to communicate back to parents. Use typed event payloads and document expected event shapes to reduce integration errors. Throttle or debounce frequent events to avoid performance problems in complex UIs.

children.title: Component Lifecycle
children.content: A component's lifecycle is the sequence of steps between creation and destruction. You can implement hooks like `ngOnInit`, `ngOnChanges`, and `ngOnDestroy`. Examples include afterEveryRender and afterNextRender for interaction with the DOM. Use lifecycle hooks sparingly and prefer reactive patterns when possible; many side-effects can be handled with signals and effects. Always clean up subscriptions and resources in `ngOnDestroy` to avoid leaks.

documentation.title: Templates
documentation.description: Angular template syntax, binding and control flow.

children.title: Template Binding
children.content: Bindings create a dynamic connection between data and templates. This includes interpolation {{}}, property binding [], and event binding (). Double curly braces {{ }} are used for text interpolation. Angular tracks signals read in templates to update the page. Keep templates declarative and simple—move business logic into the component class or computed signals. Avoid heavy computations in bindings to prevent rendering overhead and maintain responsive UIs.

sections.title: CSS class and style property bindings

children.title: Control Flow
children.content: Angular templates support @if, @for, and @switch blocks for conditional rendering and looping. These built-in blocks replace the older *ngIf and *ngFor directives. Modern Angular uses @inline blocks for control flow. Prefer clarity and readability when composing nested control-flow constructs. Use track identifiers in loops to preserve DOM state and improve rendering performance.

sections.title: Why is track in @for blocks important?

children.title: Angular Signals
children.content: Signals are a system that tracks how and where state is used. They include Writable Signals, Computed Signals, and Effects. Signals provide a reactive way to track state changes. Writable signals can be updated via set() or update(), while computed signals derive values lazily. Signals enable predictable change propagation and simplify reasoning about when parts of your UI should update. Adopt signals incrementally to modernize reactivity with minimal disruption to existing code.

children.title: DI in Angular
children.content: DI is a pattern for sharing code by injecting features into different parts of the application. Components and services participate in DI via the inject() function. A dependency is an object a class needs to work. Angular uses DI to provide these values to components and services. Design services around single responsibility and inject them at the appropriate scope to control lifetime and sharing. Prefer constructor-less `inject()` for fine-grained control in functions and standalone components when appropriate.

children.title: Define routes
children.content: Routes define which component renders for a specific URL path. In Angular, you provide a Routes array to the provideRouter function. The Angular Router enables navigation by interpreting browser URLs as instructions to change the view. Use route guards and resolvers to protect and prefetch data for routes. Keep routing configuration modular to support lazy loading and clear separation between feature areas.

documentation.title: Forms
documentation.description: Handling user input with Angular forms.

children.title: Forms in Angular
children.content: Angular provides Reactive and Template-driven approaches. Reactive forms offer explicit access to the form model, while Template-driven forms use directives like ngModel. Choosing the right approach depends on the form complexity. Validate inputs early and provide clear user feedback. Leverage typed forms and custom validators for complex scenarios to improve developer ergonomics and runtime safety.

children.title: Setting up HttpClient
children.content: You must configure HttpClient using provideHttpClient() in your application config. It supports features like withFetch, interceptors, and JSONP. Configure interceptors for concerns like authentication, logging, and error handling. Keep API contracts consistent and use typed request/response models for safer HTTP interactions.

children.title: Making HTTP requests
children.content: HttpClient provides methods for GET, POST, PUT, DELETE. They return cold Observables that only execute the request upon subscription. Handle errors explicitly and follow idempotency rules for unsafe methods. Use centralized services to coordinate HTTP interactions and caching strategies to improve performance and resilience.
